title: Hydropower Breakthrough
alt: A hydroelectric dam is also known as a heavy water reactor.
transcript:
[Beret Guy is standing on a podium behind a lectern. He is gesturing with his hand, palm up, towards a poster hanging behind him. On it is a picture of a tall dam, with a lake behind, and water coming out at the foot of the dam in the valley on the other side. Two voices reacts to Beret Guy's statement from off-panel.]
Beret Guy: We are pleased to announce that our hydroelectric dam has achieved Q>1, producing more water than we fed into it!
Off-panel voice 1: Hooray!
Off-panel voice 2: Wait.
explanation:
In this comic, Beret Guy announces that a  has reached "Q > 1". This has two possible meanings, and the humour comes from their juxtaposition.

In fluid dynamics, the letter Q represents the , or volume of fluid per unit time, e.g. m³/s. Depending on the units chosen, it would not be at all surprising for this number to be greater than 1 for a hydroelectric dam.

However, Beret Guy's clarification, that the meaning of this is that the dam is producing more water than was fed into it, suggests that he is interpreting the letter Q in a manner similar to its use in , where it represents the ratio of output power to input power. Typically fusion reactors require more power than they generate, but on the day after this comic was released, the US National Ignition Facility announced the first Q > 1 fusion reaction. However, hydroelectric dams work quite differently from fusion reactors, and (despite frequent comparisons) water is different from electricity.

If a dam were indeed producing more water than came into it from the reservoir and other sources, especially on a consistent basis (and not just because of water that had been stored somehow inside the dam) it would have to be doing so by chemical reaction. Anything else would be a violation of the law of conservation of mass (although considering who is presenting, a violation of the laws of physics is entirely possible). For example, the dam might be catalyzing hydrogen gas to burn with oxygen in the air. Such a reaction would be dangerous to the dam. A reaction might even consume the material of the dam itself.

It appears that one of the audience members is oblivious to this fact, joining in Beret Guy's celebration, while another audience member is more effectively applying their critical thinking skills.

Alternatively, it is possible that Beret Guy is suggesting that the amount of water coming out of the dam is greater than the amount travelling into it via the . If so, this indicates that water is finding other paths through the dam (a literal "breakthrough"), which is a very dangerous situation and would also be a cause for concern, not celebration.

The title text further confuses the issue by equating the hydroelectric dam with a , which is a type of nuclear fission (not fusion) reactor that uses deuterium oxide, or "heavy water", as a moderator. A hydroelectric dam is powered by the weight of water, but it is not supposed to be a reactor.
